content:
# Leviticus Chapter 22 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71r–71v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_022_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_022_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 108–109 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_022_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_022_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 22 is the companion chapter to Leviticus 21. Where chapter 21 governed the priestly body — who may serve at the altar, in what physical condition, and under what domestic arrangements — chapter 22 governs the priestly portion: who may eat the holy food, and what sacrificial animals may be presented. The chapter falls into four major sections: (1) laws governing priestly access to the holy things — qodshei benei-Yisra'el — with emphasis on conditions of ritual purity and the exclusion of the zar ("outsider"), vv.1–9; (2) laws determining who in the priestly household may eat the holy things — including slaves, but excluding hired servants, outsiders, and a priest's daughter married to a non-priest (with provisions for her return upon widowhood or divorce) — vv.10–16; (3) laws governing the acceptability of sacrificial animals — the requirement of tamim ("without blemish/complete"), the minimum age of eight days, the prohibition of the same-day slaughter of a mother-animal and its offspring (oto ve-et-beno), and the thank-offering's same-day consumption requirement — vv.17–30; and (4) the closing theological summary: "you shall not profane my holy name" (ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi) and the self-identification formula "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" — vv.31–33. The chapter's governing logic is the holiness map already traced in chapters 17–21: gradated access to sacred things corresponds to gradated proximity to divine presence. The same tamim ("without blemish/complete") demanded of the priestly body in chapter 21 is here demanded of the sacrificial animal; the same concern for profanation (challal) of the LORD's name runs throughout. The chapter concludes as the Holiness Code regularly does — with the self-identification "I am the LORD" — but here amplified: "I am the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God; I am the LORD" (v.33). The Exodus-redemption grounds the entire sacrificial-holiness system.]*

---

## Leviticus 22

**22:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**22:2** Speak to Aharon and to his sons, and they shall separate themselves [ve-yinnazeru — וְיִנָּזְרוּ; Nifal of n-z-r, "to keep away/separate/consecrate oneself"] from the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el [mi-qodshei benei-Yisra'el — מִקָּדְשֵׁי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל], which they consecrate to me, and they shall not profane [ve-lo yechalelu — וְלֹא יְחַלְּלוּ] my holy name [et-shem qodshi — אֶת-שֵׁם קָדְשִׁי]; I am the LORD.

**22:3** Say to them: Any man of all your offspring [ish ish mi-kol-zar'akhem — אִישׁ אִישׁ מִכָּל-זַרְעֲכֶם] throughout their generations [le-dorotam — לְדֹרֹתָם] who approaches [asher yiqqrav — אֲשֶׁר יִקְרַב] the holy things [el-ha-qodashim — אֶל-הַקֳּדָשִׁים] that the sons of Yisra'el consecrate to the LORD, while his uncleanness is upon him [ve-tumato alav — וְטֻמְאָתוֹ עָלָיו] — that person shall be cut off [ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi — וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא] from before my face [mi-lefanai — מִלְּפָנָי]; I am the LORD.

**22:4** Any man of the offspring of Aharon who is a skin-diseased person [asher yihyeh tsaru'a — אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה-צָרוּעַ; tsaru'a — traditionally "leprous" but encompassing various skin conditions] or who has a discharge [o zav — אוֹ זָב] shall not eat of the holy things [lo yokhal min-ha-qodashim — לֹא יֹאכַל בַּקֳּדָשִׁים] until he is clean [ad asher yithar — עַד אֲשֶׁר יִטְהָר]. And whoever touches anything unclean by a dead body [ha-noga bi-khol-tame-nefesh — הַנֹּגֵעַ בְּכָל-טְמֵא-נָפֶשׁ; lit. "one touching anything unclean of a soul/corpse"], or a man from whom a discharge of seed [o ish asher tetse mimenu shich'vat-zara — אוֹ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-תֵּצֵא מִמֶּנּוּ שִׁכְבַת-זָרַע] goes out —

**22:5** or a man who touches any swarming thing [o ish asher yiga be-khol-sherets — אוֹ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר יִגַּע בְּכָל-שֶׁרֶץ] by which he becomes unclean [asher yitma-lo — אֲשֶׁר יִטְמָא-לוֹ], or in a man [o be-adam — אוֹ בְאָדָם] who makes him unclean by any uncleanness of his [asher yetamme-lo le-khol tumato — אֲשֶׁר יְטַמֵּא-לוֹ לְכֹל טֻמְאָתוֹ] —

**22:6** the person [ha-nefesh — הַנֶּפֶשׁ] who touches any such thing shall be unclean until the evening [tamei ad-ha-arev — טָמֵא עַד-הָעָרֶב] and shall not eat of the holy things [ve-lo yokhal min-ha-qodashim — וְלֹא יֹאכַל מִן-הַקֳּדָשִׁים] unless he has bathed his flesh in water [ki im-rachats besaro ba-mayim — כִּי אִם-רָחַץ בְּשָׂרוֹ בַּמָּיִם].

**22:7** And when the sun sets [u-va ha-shemesh — וּבָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ] he shall be clean [ve-taher — וְטָהֵר], and afterward he may eat of the holy things [ve-achar yokhal min-ha-qodashim — וְאַחַר יֹאכַל מִן-הַקֳּדָשִׁים], for it is his food [ki lachmo hu — כִּי לַחְמוֹ הוּא].

**22:8** A carcass [nevelah — נְבֵלָה; an animal that died without proper slaughter] or one torn [u-terefah — וּטְרֵפָה; an animal killed by another animal] he shall not eat, to make himself unclean by it [la-tame-vah bo — לְטָמְאָה-בָהּ בָּהּ]; I am the LORD.

**22:9** And they shall keep my charge [ve-shamru et-mishmarti — וְשָׁמְרוּ אֶת-מִשְׁמַרְתִּי], and they shall not bear sin for it [ve-lo yis'u alav chet — וְלֹא-יִשְׂאוּ עָלָיו חֵטְא] and die for it [va-metu vo — וָמֵתוּ בוֹ] when they profane it [ki yechalelluhu — כִּי יְחַלְּלֻהוּ]; I am the LORD who sanctifies them [meqadsham — מְקַדְּשָׁם].

**22:10** And no outsider [ve-khol-zar — וְכָל-זָר; zar — "stranger/outsider," any non-priest] shall eat a holy thing [lo yokhal qodesh — לֹא-יֹאכַל קֹדֶשׁ]; a tenant of a priest [toshav kohen — תּוֹשַׁב כֹּהֵן; a resident worker who has not been purchased as a slave] or a hired worker [ve-sachir — וְשָׂכִיר] shall not eat a holy thing.

**22:11** But if a priest buys a person [ve-kohen ki-yiqneh nefesh — וְכֹהֵן כִּי-יִקְנֶה נֶפֶשׁ] with his silver [qinyan kaspo — קִנְיַן כַּסְפּוֹ] — he [the purchased person] may eat of it [hu yokhal bo — הוּא יֹאכַל-בּוֹ]; and one born of his house [u-ylid beito — וִילִיד בֵּיתוֹ; a slave born into the household] — they may eat of his bread [hem yokhelu be-lachmo — הֵם יֹאכְלוּ בְלַחְמוֹ].

**22:12** And the daughter of a priest [u-vat-ish kohen — וּבַת-אִישׁ כֹּהֵן], if she becomes the wife of an outsider [ki tihyeh le-ish zar — כִּי תִהְיֶה לְאִישׁ זָר] — she shall not eat of the contribution of the holy things [hi bi-trumat ha-qodashim lo tokhal — הִיא בִּתְרוּמַת הַקֳּדָשִׁים לֹא תֹאכֵל; terumah — the "contribution/heave offering" set aside for the priests].

**22:13** But if the daughter of a priest is widowed [ve-vat-ish kohen ki tihyeh almanah — וּבַת-אִישׁ כֹּהֵן כִּי תִהְיֶה אַלְמָנָה] or divorced [o gerushah — אוֹ גְרוּשָׁה] and has no offspring [ve-zera ein lah — וְזֶרַע אֵין-לָהּ] and returns to her father's house [ve-shavah el-beit avihah — וְשָׁבָה אֶל-בֵּית אָבִיהָ] as in her youth [ki-vine'ureha — כִּבִנְעוּרֶיהָ] — from her father's bread [mi-lechem avihah — מִלֶּחֶם אָבִיהָ] she may eat; but no outsider [ve-khol-zar — וְכָל-זָר] shall eat of it.

**22:14** And a man who eats a holy thing inadvertently [ve-ish ki-yokhal qodesh bi-shgagah — וְאִישׁ כִּי-יֹאכַל קֹדֶשׁ בִּשְׁגָגָה; bi-shgagah — "in inadvertence/by error"] shall add its fifth to it [ve-yasaf chamishito alav — וְיָסַף חֲמִישִׁתוֹ עָלָיו] and give the holy thing to the priest [ve-natan la-kohen et-ha-qodesh — וְנָתַן לַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַקֹּדֶשׁ].

**22:15** And they shall not profane [ve-lo yechalelu — וְלֹא יְחַלְּלוּ] the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el [et qodshei benei-Yisra'el — אֵת קָדְשֵׁי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] which they contribute [asher yarimu — אֲשֶׁר יָרִימוּ] to the LORD,

**22:16** and cause them to bear the iniquity of guilt [ve-hishilu otam avon ashmah — וְהִשִּׂיאוּ אוֹתָם עֲוֹן אַשְׁמָה] when they eat their holy things [be-akholam et-qodsheihem — בְּאָכְלָם אֶת-קָדְשֵׁיהֶם]; for I am the LORD who sanctifies them [ki ani YHWH meqadsham — כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה מְקַדְּשָׁם].

**22:17** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**22:18** Speak to Aharon and to his sons and to all the sons of Yisra'el and say to them: Any man [ish ish — אִישׁ אִישׁ; the doubled form emphasizes universality] from the house of Yisra'el or from the sojourners [min-ha-ger — מִן-הַגֵּר] in Yisra'el who presents his offering [asher yaqriv qorbano — אֲשֶׁר יַקְרִיב קָרְבָּנוֹ] for any of their vows [le-khol nidreihem — לְכָל-נִדְרֵיהֶם] or for any of their freewill offerings [u-le-khol-nidvotam — וּלְכָל-נִדְבוֹתָם] which they present to the LORD for a burnt offering [asher-yaqrivu la-YHWH le-olah — אֲשֶׁר-יַקְרִיבוּ לַיהוָה לְעֹלָה] —

**22:19** for your acceptance [li-retsone'khem — לִרְצֹנְכֶם; li-retson — "for your being accepted/for favor"], a male without blemish [zachar tamim — זָכָר תָּמִים; tamim — "complete, whole, without blemish"] from the cattle [min-ha-bakar — מִן-הַבָּקָר], from the sheep [min-ha-kesavim — מִן-הַכְּשָׂבִים], or from the goats [u-min-ha-izzim — וּמִן-הָעִזִּים].

**22:20** Anything in which there is a blemish [asher-bo mum — אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ מוּם; mum — "defect/blemish"] you shall not present [lo taqrivu — לֹא תַקְרִיבוּ], for it will not be for acceptance for you [ki lo le-ratson yihyeh lakhem — כִּי לֹא-לְרָצוֹן יִהְיֶה לָכֶם].

**22:21** And a man who presents [ve-ish ki-yaqriv — וְאִישׁ כִּי-יַקְרִיב] a peace offering [zevach shelamim — זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים] to the LORD to fulfill a vow [le-fallei neder — לְפַלֵּא-נֶדֶר; le-fallei — "to make extraordinary/to fulfill a special vow"] or for a freewill offering [o li-ndavah — אוֹ לִנְדָבָה] from the cattle or from the flock [min-ha-bakar o min-ha-tson — מִן-הַבָּקָר אוֹ-מִן-הַצֹּאן] — without blemish it shall be [tamim yihyeh — תָּמִים יִהְיֶה] for acceptance [le-ratson — לְרָצוֹן]; no blemish shall be in it [kol-mum lo-yihyeh vo — כָּל-מוּם לֹא-יִהְיֶה-בוֹ].

**22:22** The blind or broken [iver o shavur — עִוֵּר אוֹ שָׁבוּר] or maimed or having a running sore [o-charutz o-yabbelet — אוֹ-חָרוּץ אוֹ-יַבֶּלֶת; charutz — "incised/gashed/slit"; yabbelet — "a wart or festering growth"] or scabby or with a skin discharge [o garav o yalefet — אוֹ גָרָב אוֹ יַלֶּפֶת] — these you shall not present to the LORD [lo taqrivu elleh la-YHWH — לֹא-תַקְרִיבוּ אֵלֶּה לַיהוָה] and a fire-offering you shall not place from them [ve-isheh lo-titenu meihem — וְאִשֶּׁה לֹא-תִתְּנוּ מֵהֶם] upon the altar to the LORD [al-ha-mizbeach la-YHWH — עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה].

**22:23** And an ox or a sheep with one limb extended or contracted [u-shor ve-seh sarua ve-qalutt — וְשׁוֹר וָשֶׂה שָׂרוּעַ וְקָלוּט; sarua — "one limb disproportionately elongated"; qalutt — "one limb shrunken/contracted/fused"] — for a freewill offering [li-ndavah — לִנְדָבָה] you may prepare it [ta'aseh oto — תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתוֹ], but for a vow it will not be accepted [ve-le-neder lo yeratsah — וּלְנֶדֶר לֹא יֵרָצֶה].

**22:24** And that which has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut [u-machutz u-kathit u-nathiq ve-kharet — וּמָעוּךְ וְכָתוּת וְנָתוּק וְכָרוּת; machutz — "bruised/mashed"; kathit — "crushed/beaten"; nathiq — "torn away"; kharet — "cut off"] you shall not present to the LORD [lo taqrivu la-YHWH — לֹא תַקְרִיבוּ לַיהוָה]; and in your land [u-ve-artsekhem — וּבְאַרְצְכֶם] you shall not do this [lo ta'asu — לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ].

**22:25** And from the hand of a foreigner's son [u-mi-yad ben-nekhar — וּמִיַּד בֶּן-נֵכָר; ben-nekhar — "a son of a foreign people," a non-Israelite] you shall not present the bread of your God [lo taqrivu et-lechem Elohekhem — לֹא תַקְרִיבוּ אֶת-לֶחֶם אֱלֹהֵיכֶם] from any of these [mi-kol-elleh — מִכָּל-אֵלֶּה], for their corruption is in them [ki mashchatam bahem — כִּי מָשְׁחָתָם בָּהֶם; mashchat — "their ruin/corruption/deformity"], a blemish is in them [mum bam — מוּם בָּם]; they will not be accepted for you [lo yiratseu lakhem — לֹא יֵרָצוּ לָכֶם].

**22:26** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**22:27** When an ox or a sheep or a goat is born [shor o kesev o ez ki yivvaled — שׁוֹר אוֹ-כֶשֶׂב אוֹ-עֵז כִּי יִוָּלֵד] — it shall be seven days [ve-hayah shivat yamim — וְהָיָה שִׁבְעַת-יָמִים] under its mother [tachat immo — תַּחַת אִמּוֹ], and from the eighth day onward [u-mi-yom ha-shemini va-hal'ah — וּמִיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי וָהָלְאָה] it will be accepted [yiratsah — יֵרָצֶה] as a fire-offering [le-isheh — לְאִשֶּׁה] to the LORD.

**22:28** And an ox [ve-shor — וְשׁוֹר] or a sheep [o-seh — אוֹ-שֶׂה] — it and its offspring [oto ve-et-beno — אֹתוֹ וְאֶת-בְּנוֹ] you shall not slaughter [lo tishchatu — לֹא תִשְׁחֲטוּ] on the same day [be-yom echad — בְּיוֹם אֶחָד].

**22:29** And when you sacrifice a thank offering [ve-chi tizbechu zevach todah — וְכִי תִזְבְּחוּ זֶבַח-תּוֹדָה; todah — "thank offering/confession offering"] to the LORD [la-YHWH — לַיהוָה] — for your acceptance you shall sacrifice it [li-rtsone'khem tizbechu — לִרְצֹנְכֶם תִּזְבָּחֻהוּ].

**22:30** On that very day [be-yom ha-hu — בְּיוֹם הַהוּא] it shall be eaten [ye'akhel — יֵאָכֵל]; you shall not leave any of it [lo totiru mimmennu — לֹא תוֹתִירוּ מִמֶּנּוּ] until morning [ad-boker — עַד-בֹּקֶר]; I am the LORD.

**22:31** And you shall keep [u-shmartem — וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם] my commandments [et-mitsvotai — אֶת-מִצְוֹתַי] and do them [va-asitem otam — וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם]; I am the LORD.

**22:32** And you shall not profane my holy name [ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi — וְלֹא תְחַלְּלוּ אֶת-שֵׁם קָדְשִׁי], and I will be sanctified [ve-niqdashhti — וְנִקְדַּשְׁתִּי; Nifal — "I will be treated as holy/I will be hallowed"] in the midst of the sons of Yisra'el [be-tokh benei-Yisra'el — בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל]; I am the LORD who sanctifies you [ani YHWH meqadishkhem — אֲנִי יְהוָה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם],

**22:33** who brought you out [ha-motsi etkhem — הַמּוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם] from the land of Egypt [me-erets Mitsrayim — מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם] to be your God [li-hyot lakhem l'Elohim — לִהְיוֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים]; I am the LORD [ani YHWH — אֲנִי יְהוָה].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview

Leviticus 22 is the practical companion to the priestly-holiness legislation of chapter 21. Where chapter 21 governed the priest's body, mourning practices, marriage, and bodily wholeness required for altar-service, chapter 22 governs two related domains: (1) who may eat the qodashim — the holy portions — and under what conditions of purity; and (2) what animals may be presented as sacrificial offerings. The chapter is tightly organized around the concept of tamim ("without blemish/complete") as a quality demanded both of the sacrificial animal and, implicitly (by parallel with chapter 21), of the priest who serves. The verb challal ("to profane") is the chapter's governing negative term, appearing in vv.2, 9, 15, and climactically in v.32 — the prohibition of chillul ha-Shem (profanation of the Name) — which gives the chapter its theological apex. The closing triplet (vv.31–33) recapitulates the Holiness Code's structural logic: mitsvah → sanctification of the Name → Exodus-grounding of the covenant relationship. The LORD who sanctifies Israel is the same LORD who redeemed Israel from Egypt; holiness and redemption are not separable themes but aspects of a single covenantal identity.

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v.2 — ve-yinnazeru mi-qodshei benei-Yisra'el:** The verb yinnazeru is the Nifal of n-z-r — the root that gives us nazir ("Nazirite," the consecrated one who separates himself). Here it is used for the priests: "they shall separate themselves from the holy things." This is one of the most precise reversals in the Holiness Code: the same root (n-z-r) that elsewhere denotes special consecrated dedication now governs the act of withdrawing from contact with holy things when ritually unqualified. The paradox is deliberate — even the consecrated must "separate" from the holy when unclean, because unholy contact with holy things profanes both. The verse also functions as a topic sentence for the entire first section (vv.1–9): the specific conditions under which priests must separate from the qodashim follow in vv.3–9.

**v.3 — ish ish mi-kol-zar'akhem:** The doubled ish ish ("any man, man") is a Hebrew idiom of distributive universality: "every single man among all your offspring." The phrasing encompasses the entire priestly lineage across all generations (le-dorotam). The threat is karet — "cutting off" — the same formula used throughout the Holiness Code for the gravest violations (cf. 17:4, 9, 10, 14; 18:29; 19:8; 20:3, 5, 6, 17, 18). Here it is notably mi-lefanai — "from before my face" — a formulation that emphasizes the relational dimension of the exclusion: not merely death or social excision, but removal from the divine presence that the priestly office is meant to serve.

**v.4 — tsaru'a / zav:** The two chronic states of ritual disqualification named here are the tsara'at-affliction (tsaru'a — encompassing various skin conditions governed by Lev 13–14) and the zav condition (a man with an ongoing penile discharge, governed by Lev 15:1–15). Both represent conditions of ongoing, chronic impurity that are not resolved by mere bathing and waiting; they require extended purification processes. A priest afflicted with these conditions is doubly restricted: he already bore the social-religious disabilities described in Lev 13–15 for any Israelite; chapter 22 makes explicit that he additionally cannot eat of the priestly portions. The verse then lists further conditions (vv.4b–5) that also disqualify even without these chronic states.

**v.4b — tame-nefesh:** The phrase tamei-nefesh (lit. "unclean of soul/person") is a technical term for corpse-impurity — the defilement that comes from contact with a dead human body, the most potent source of ritual impurity in the Levitical system. It is the same impurity governed by the elaborated laws of Num 19 (the red heifer purification). The phrase shich'vat-zara (lit. "a lying of seed" — i.e., a seminal emission) refers to the condition governed by Lev 15:16–18, which imposes a one-day uncleanness on any man experiencing emission.

**v.5 — sherets:** The swarming things (sheratsim) that cause impurity are enumerated in Lev 11:29–38. Contact with their carcasses produces a lesser, one-day impurity resolved by bathing and sunset (v.6). The phrase "in a man who makes him unclean by any uncleanness of his" encompasses the various forms of human-transmitted impurity (contact with a zav, zavah, or other unclean person — Lev 15).

**v.7 — u-va ha-shemesh ve-taher:** "When the sun sets and he shall be clean" — the formula reveals the basic temporal logic of minor priestly impurity: it lasts until sunset, after which (and after ritual bathing) the priest returns to full status. The phrase "for it is his food" (ki lachmo hu) is poignant: the priestly portions are not optional extras but the livelihood — the bread — of the priestly household. The law thus balances purity-maintenance against practical sustenance: the priest must not eat while unclean, but the system ensures he can eat as soon as the impurity is resolved.

**v.8 — nevelah u-terefah:** The nevelah (carcass of an animal that died without proper slaughter) and the terefah (animal killed or mortally wounded by predators — from the root t-r-f, "to tear") are the two principal categories of prohibited animal consumption throughout the Torah (cf. Exod 22:30; Deut 14:21; Lev 17:15). An ordinary Israelite who eats nevelah or terefah becomes unclean until evening and must launder his garments (Lev 17:15); the priest is told flatly: "he shall not eat [it] to make himself unclean by it." The priestly prohibition is absolute; the ordinary Israelite's prohibition involves consequences but is not stated as categorically. The phrase la-tame-vah bo ("to make himself unclean by it") uses the double-lamed construction to underscore that eating such food is itself an act of deliberate self-defilement — cognate with the volitional Nifal t-m-' seen throughout the priestly legislation.

**v.9 — mishmarti / lo yis'u alav chet:** "They shall keep my charge" — mishmarti (from shomer, "to keep/guard") is the standard term for priestly vigilance over cultic regulations. "They shall not bear sin for it" — the phrase nasa chet (lit. "to bear/carry sin") means to incur guilt that then "rests on" the person; the sin is not abstract but a real burden with real consequences. "And die for it" — the penalty for priestly violation of purity-regulations in contact with holy things is death (cf. Lev 10:1–2, Nadab and Abihu; Num 4:15, 20). The final phrase "when they profane it" (ki yechalelluhu) — with the object suffix referring to mishmarti ("my charge") — is unusual; it suggests that to fail in keeping the priestly charge is itself an act of profanation, not merely a procedural lapse.

**v.10 — zar:** The term zar ("outsider/stranger") is the chapter's key restrictive term for priestly access. It does not mean a foreigner (the term for that is nokri or ben-nekhar, as in v.25) but any non-priest — any Israelite or sojourner who is not of Aaronid descent and therefore not entitled to priestly portions. Three categories of zar are excluded: the generic zar (any outsider), the toshav kohen (a priest's resident tenant, a free man who lives with the priestly household but has not been purchased), and the sachir (a hired worker employed on wages). Two categories are permitted: the qinyan kaspo (one bought with the priest's silver — a slave) and the ylid beito (one born into the priestly household — a house-born slave). The logic is household-membership: the slave is fully incorporated into the priestly domestic unit; the hired man and the tenant are not. The slave's eating of priestly food does not profane it; the outsider's eating does.

**v.12 — trumat ha-qodashim:** The terumah (contribution/heave offering) is the portion elevated (harim — "to lift up") from the sacrificial offering and given to the priests (Lev 7:14, 32–34; Num 18:8–19). The phrase bi-trumat ha-qodashim ("of the contribution of the holy things") specifies this category of priestly portion as what the non-priestly husband disqualifies the priest's daughter from sharing. The priest's daughter who marries a non-priest exits the priestly household's domain of holy food, even though she was born into it. Upon returning (widowhood, childless divorce) she re-enters it: she returns ke-vine'ureha ("as in her youth"), which restores her original status. The phrase is legal-technical and emotionally resonant: she is received back into the father's house in her pre-marriage state.

**v.14 — bi-shgagah / chamishito:** "Inadvertently" — bi-shgagah (from shagag, "to err/go astray") is the standard Levitical term for unintentional violation, distinguished from willful (be-yad ramah — "with a high hand") transgression. Unintentional eating of holy food requires restitution: the value of what was eaten plus an additional fifth (chamishito — "its fifth"; the Levitical "fifth" is 20%, making the total restoration 120%). The same formula applies to unintentional misuse of sanctuary property (Lev 5:16). The restitution goes to the priest whose holy food was consumed, making the priest whole while also imposing a penalty that acknowledges the violation.

**v.15 — ve-lo yechalelu:** "They shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el" — the subject is ambiguous. It may refer to the priests (who would profane the holy things by eating them in impurity or allowing outsiders to eat them), or it may refer to the sons of Yisra'el (who would profane the holy things by eating them as non-priests). The following verse (v.16) resolves the ambiguity: the profanation in view is the causing of guilt (avon ashmah) upon "them" by allowing unauthorized eating. The verb nasa avon ("to bear iniquity") combined with ashm ("guilt") creates the compound avon ashmah — a guilt-carrying iniquity — that is heavier than either term alone.

**v.18 — ish ish / u-min-ha-ger:** The doubled ish ish again signals comprehensive universality. Crucially, the sacrificial-animal laws of vv.17–25 are explicitly extended to the ger — the sojourner/resident alien in Yisra'el. The ger who brings a sacrifice to the LORD must meet the same tamim requirement as any Israelite. This is an important extension of the sacrificial system to non-Israelite worshipers who have attached themselves to the community: they may bring offerings, but the offerings must meet the same standards. The categories of acceptable offerings are the 'olah (burnt offering, fully consumed on the altar), the neder (vow offering), and the nedavah (freewill offering).

**v.19 — zachar tamim:** "A male without blemish" — the tamim requirement encompasses the animal's physical completeness and wholeness. The root t-m-m (complete, whole, without lack or defect) is the same root that appears in the covenant formula tamim tihyeh im-YHWH Elohekha (Deut 18:13 — "you shall be complete/blameless with the LORD your God") and in Abraham's instruction to walk before God ve-heyei tamim (Gen 17:1). The word carries both physical and moral-covenantal resonance. In the sacrificial context it is definitively physical: the animal must have no bodily defect. The insistence on a male animal for the 'olah and the shelamim-vow aligns with Lev 1:3 ('olah from the cattle: zachar tamim) and 3:1 (shelamim: zachar o neqevah, but tamim throughout).

**v.20 — lo le-ratson yihyeh lakhem:** "It will not be for acceptance for you" — ratson (from ratsah, "to be pleased with/to accept favorably") is the key term for sacrificial acceptability throughout the Holiness Code (cf. 1:3, 4; 7:18; 19:5; 22:19, 20, 21, 23, 25, 27, 29). A sacrifice that does not produce ratson — divine favor/acceptance — is worse than no sacrifice: it is an offering that fails at its essential purpose, and the failure is not neutral but profaning. The phrase ki lo le-ratson is blunt: no "perhaps" or conditional; a blemished animal simply will not be accepted.

**v.21 — le-fallei neder:** The phrase le-fallei neder (from pala, "to be extraordinary/to make distinct") denotes the fulfillment of a special or solemn vow — one that has been formally declared in a cultic setting. This is the vow-offering (neder), distinct from the freewill offering (nedavah) in that the neder is obligatory once declared (failure to fulfill it constitutes sin: Deut 23:22–24), while the nedavah is purely voluntary. The pairing of neder and nedavah occurs frequently in the sacrificial legislation as a comprehensive binary covering all voluntary presentations to the sanctuary.

**v.22 — iver o shavur o charutz o yabbelet:** The blemish list for animals in vv.22–24 parallels the list for priests in 21:17–23. The correspondence is structurally significant: as the priest offering must be tamim, so the animal offered must be tamim; neither the blemished body of the offerer nor the blemished body of the offering can approach the altar. The specific terms in v.22 are: iver ("blind"), shavur ("broken/fractured"), charutz ("incised/gashed" — LXX: kollobirinon, a difficult term; possibly an animal with a cleft or slashed mark on the ear or nose), yabbelet ("a wart or swelling growth"), garav (the same skin-disease term used in the priestly blemish list, 21:20 — a festering sore or eruption), and yalefet (also from 21:20 — a dry scabby skin condition). The last two terms (garav, yalefet) create an exact lexical parallel with the priestly blemish list, underscoring the structural homology: animal and priest must each be tamim.

**v.22b — ve-isheh lo-titenu meihem al-ha-mizbeach:** "And a fire-offering you shall not place from them upon the altar" — the distinction between presenting (haqriv — to bring near/offer) and placing as fire-offering (natan le-isheh — to give as a fire-offering) is subtle but real. Some blemished animals might be brought to the sanctuary precincts without being fully disqualified from all uses; but as an isheh (fire-offering, the altar-portion consumed by fire) they are absolutely prohibited. The prohibition encompasses both the private presentation to the priest and the public altar-sacrifice.

**v.23 — sarua ve-qalutt:** The pairing sarua ve-qalutt describes an animal with one limb either disproportionately extended (sarua — root s-r-', to spread out/elongate) or one limb shrunken and fused (qalutt — from the root q-l-t, to diminish/fuse). The LXX renders this as monokoton kai kolobon — "one-cut and shortened/docked" — suggesting the Greek understood these as conditions affecting overall limb-count or relative proportion. The critical legal distinction in v.23 is between the nedavah (freewill offering) and the neder (vow): an animal with sarua ve-qalutt may be brought as a freewill offering (where no specific animal was declared) but will not be accepted for a vow-offering (where the animal presented must match what was vowed). This is the chapter's only legal accommodation for a blemished animal — and it is a narrow one, applying only to this specific defect and only for the most voluntary category of offering.

**v.24 — machutz ve-khathit ve-nathiq ve-kharet:** The four terms all describe injuries to the testicles with progressively greater severity: machutz ("bruised/mashed" — compressed), kathit ("beaten/crushed" — reduced to pulp by impact), nathiq ("torn away" — avulsed), kharet ("cut off" — castrated). These conditions collectively describe castration in its various forms, whether by crushing, tearing, or incision. The law prohibits presenting such an animal and then extends the prohibition: "and in your land you shall not do this" — lo ta'asu. The phrase seems to prohibit the act of castrating animals for sacrificial or other purposes within the land of Yisra'el, not merely the offering of castrated animals. The text addresses a practice: some cultures castrated animals to improve their quality as food or offerings; this is categorically forbidden.

**v.25 — ben-nekhar / mashchatam bahem:** The ben-nekhar ("son of a foreign people") designates a non-Israelite who is not a resident ger (sojourner integrated into the community) but a true foreigner. While v.18 permits the ger to bring offerings (subject to the same tamim requirements), the ben-nekhar may not present offerings at all — not because of prejudice but because the offerings of the ben-nekhar cannot meet the required standard: "their corruption/deformity is in them" (mashchatam bahem — from shahat, "to ruin/destroy/corrupt"). The term mashchat here refers not necessarily to moral corruption but to a structural defect that disqualifies the offering. The verse implies that offerings brought from foreign sources through foreigners carry an inherent disqualification.

**v.27 — shivat yamim tachat immo:** "Seven days under its mother" — the minimum age requirement of seven days (making the eighth day the first acceptable day for sacrifice: u-mi-yom ha-shemini va-hal'ah — "from the eighth day and onward it will be accepted") has ancient parallels. The first seven days of a newborn animal's life represent a period of vulnerability and dependency; the animal is not yet fully constituted as an independent living creature during this time. The eighth day is the threshold of established life — parallel to the eighth-day circumcision that marks the full entry of a male child into the covenant (Gen 17:12; Lev 12:3). The convergence is probably not coincidental: the eighth day is the threshold of covenant-eligibility for humans and the threshold of sacrificial eligibility for animals.

**v.28 — oto ve-et-beno lo tishchatu be-yom echad:** "It and its offspring you shall not slaughter on the same day" — this is one of the Torah's explicit animal-compassion laws (alongside Deut 22:6–7, the prohibition of taking a mother-bird with her young). The phrase oto ve-et-beno ("it and its offspring") technically refers to mother and child; the pronoun oto (masculine) refers to the parent animal generically (either cow or ewe), ve-et-beno ("and its son") refers to the offspring. The law prevents the emotional and symbolic brutality of slaughtering both parent and child on the same day, regardless of which is slaughtered first. Unlike the mother-bird law (which explicitly commands the mother-bird be sent away), this law makes no statement about which animal may be slaughtered first; it only prohibits both on the same day. Rabbinic tradition extended the prohibition to cover any mother-offspring pair regardless of which is slaughtered first (Chullin 78a).

**v.29 — zevach todah / li-rtsone'khem:** The todah (thank offering/acknowledgment offering) is the most immediately consumed of the shelamim-category offerings. Lev 7:15 has already established that the todah must be eaten on the day of its offering, with no remainder left until morning. Here the same rule is given in the context of a general exhortation, framing it in terms of li-retsonekhem — "for your acceptance" — the same ratson-formula used in the burnt-offering and vow-offering sections. The requirement of same-day consumption prevents the todah from being treated as an ordinary communal feast spread over days; it must be consumed in the urgency of a single day's grateful acknowledgment.

**v.32 — ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi / ve-niqdashhti:** The climactic verse of the chapter — and one of the most theologically weighted verses in the entire Holiness Code. "You shall not profane my holy name" (ve-lo techalelu et-shem qodshi) introduces the concept of chillul ha-Shem — the desecration of the divine Name — which becomes a foundational category in later Jewish theology. The Name (ha-Shem) is not merely God's designation but the totality of divine reputation, character, and self-disclosure as experienced by Israel and perceived by the nations. To profane it is to act in ways that bring the divine name into disrepute — whether through ritual violation (the context here) or ethical failure (the broader Holiness Code concern). The counterpoint is ve-niqdashhti — "I will be sanctified" (Nifal of q-d-sh, "to be treated as holy/to be hallowed"). The passive construction is striking: the LORD's sanctification is in some sense contingent on Israel's behavior. This is not a limitation of divine holiness but a statement about its relational, covenantal expression: the LORD's holiness is manifested or obscured in the life of the covenant people. The phrase be-tokh benei-Yisra'el ("in the midst of the sons of Yisra'el") locates the sanctification within the community — a communal, public event, not merely an individual spiritual experience.

**v.33 — ha-motsi etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim:** The closing verse grounds the entire legislative structure of Leviticus 22 — and by extension the whole Holiness Code — in the Exodus event. "Who brought you out from the land of Egypt to be your God; I am the LORD." The four-word formula li-hyot lakhem l'Elohim ("to be your God for you") is the covenant identification formula of the Sinai relationship: the purpose of the Exodus was not merely liberation from slavery but the establishment of a covenant identity — Israel as the people whose God is the LORD, and the LORD as the God of Israel. Every law in the Holiness Code — every tamim requirement, every purity restriction, every prohibition of profaning the Name — flows from this: the LORD who brought Israel out of Egypt is the same LORD who now defines what it means to be his people. Holiness is not an imposition but the internal logic of redeemed existence.

## Key Term Table

| Hebrew | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| וְיִנָּזְרוּ | ve-yinnazeru | "they shall separate themselves" | Nifal of n-z-r; root of nazir (Nazirite); separation from holy things when unqualified |
| קָדְשֵׁי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל | qodshei benei-Yisra'el | "the holy things of the sons of Yisra'el" | The consecrated portions dedicated to the LORD through the sacrificial system |
| כָּרֵת / וְנִכְרְתָה | karet / ve-nikhretah | "cutting off" | The severest non-capital sanction; removal from covenant community |
| מִלְּפָנַי | mi-lefanai | "from before my face" | Relational dimension of karet: exclusion from divine presence |
| צָרוּעַ | tsaru'a | "skin-diseased person" | Technical term for affliction governed by Lev 13–14; not simply "leprosy" |
| זָב | zav | "one with a discharge" | Man with chronic penile discharge; Lev 15:1–15 |
| טְמֵא-נֶפֶשׁ | tame-nefesh | "unclean by a dead body" | Corpse-impurity; the most potent ritual defilement in the Levitical system |
| שִׁכְבַת-זָרַע | shich'vat-zara | "a discharge of seed" | Seminal emission; Lev 15:16–18 |
| שֶׁרֶץ | sherets | "swarming thing" | Creeping creatures whose carcasses transmit minor impurity; Lev 11:29–38 |
| נְבֵלָה | nevelah | "carcass" | Animal that died without proper slaughter |
| טְרֵפָה | terefah | "one torn" | Animal killed or mortally wounded by predators |
| מִשְׁמַרְתִּי | mishmarti | "my charge" | The priestly obligation of watchful custody over cultic regulations |
| זָר | zar | "outsider" | Any non-priest; key exclusion term for priestly portions |
| תּוֹשַׁב | toshav | "tenant" | Free resident worker; excluded from priestly portions |
| שָׂכִיר | sachir | "hired worker" | Employee on wages; excluded from priestly portions |
| קִנְיַן כַּסְפּוֹ | qinyan kaspo | "his silver-purchase" | A bought slave; included in priestly household |
| יְלִיד בֵּיתוֹ | ylid beito | "one born of his house" | House-born slave; included in priestly household |
| תְּרוּמַת הַקֳּדָשִׁים | trumat ha-qodashim | "the contribution of the holy things" | The elevated/heave-offering portion designated for priests |
| בִּשְׁגָגָה | bi-shgagah | "inadvertently" | Unintentional violation; contrasted with willful (be-yad ramah) transgression |
| חֲמִישִׁתוֹ | chamishito | "its fifth" | 20% restitution penalty added to the principal value |
| תָּמִים | tamim | "without blemish / complete" | Core sacrificial and covenantal term; wholeness demanded of both priest and animal |
| לְרָצוֹן | le-ratson | "for acceptance / for favor" | Divine acceptance of a sacrifice; the goal of all offering |
| נֵדֶר | neder | "vow" | Formally declared obligatory offering; must be fulfilled |
| נְדָבָה | nedavah | "freewill offering" | Voluntary, undeclared offering; greater flexibility |
| שָׂרוּעַ | sarua | "one limb extended" | Disproportionately elongated limb; permitted for nedavah only |
| קָלוּט | qalutt | "one limb contracted" | Shrunken/fused limb; same restriction as sarua |
| מָעוּךְ | machutz | "bruised" | Testicle compressed/mashed; prohibits animal from offering |
| כָּתוּת | kathit | "crushed" | Testicle beaten; form of castration-injury |
| נָתוּק | nathiq | "torn away" | Testicle avulsed; form of castration-injury |
| כָּרוּת | kharet | "cut off" | Testicle excised; full castration |
| בֶּן-נֵכָר | ben-nekhar | "a son of a foreign people" | Non-Israelite foreigner; distinct from resident ger |
| גֵּר | ger | "sojourner" | Resident alien integrated into covenant community; may bring offerings |
| זֶבַח תּוֹדָה | zevach todah | "thank offering" | Most urgently consumed of the shelamim; same-day consumption required |
| אֹתוֹ וְאֶת-בְּנוֹ | oto ve-et-beno | "it and its offspring" | The animal-compassion law; mother and offspring not slaughtered same day |
| חִלּוּל הַשֵּׁם | chillul ha-Shem | "profaning the Name" | Desecration of the divine reputation; the chapter's climactic prohibition |
| וְנִקְדַּשְׁתִּי | ve-niqdashhti | "I will be sanctified" | Nifal of q-d-sh; the LORD's holiness manifested through Israel's conduct |

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 108–109)

**v.2 — ve-yinnazeru:** LXX *kai phylaxontai* — "and they shall guard/keep." The LXX renders the Nifal of n-z-r not with a separation-verb but with phylassein ("to guard"), losing the rich connotations of the nazir-root. The Greek frames the verse as a command of vigilance rather than one of withdrawal. This is a significant semantic shift: the Hebrew insists on the priests actively withdrawing from holy things when unclean; the Greek implies a more general guardedness. Vaticanus reads phylaxontai consistently at this point.

**v.3 — ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-lefanai:** LXX *exolethreuthēsetai hē psychē ekeinē ap' emou* — "that soul shall be utterly destroyed from me." The LXX uses exolothreuein ("to destroy utterly") rather than simply "cut off," intensifying the punishment. The phrase *ap' emou* ("from me") preserves the mi-lefanai ("from before my face") relational dimension of the MT.

**v.4 — tsaru'a:** LXX *lepros* — "leprous." The LXX's standard rendering of tsara'at-conditions as lepra (and the tsaru'a as lepros) has profoundly shaped subsequent Christian interpretation, creating the association with leprosy proper (Hansen's disease) when the Hebrew term is considerably broader. Vaticanus uses lepros consistently in Leviticus.

**v.4 — shich'vat-zara:** LXX *gonorrheia* — "a flow/discharge of seed." The Greek medical term gonorrheia ("seed-flow") is a precise technical rendering that entered medical terminology through the LXX's translation of this verse. The LXX is here more clinically precise than the MT's periphrastic construction.

**v.8 — nevelah u-terefah:** LXX *thnesimaion kai theriotōton* — "that which has died of itself and that which was caught/torn by wild animals." The LXX expands terefah slightly: theriotōton (from thērioo — "torn by wild beasts") makes the cause of death explicit. The MT's terefah includes any cause of violent death outside proper slaughter; the LXX narrows it to beast-attack.

**v.10 — zar:** LXX *allogenēs* — "one of another race/kind." The LXX renders zar as allogenēs throughout this section (also in vv.12, 13). The term allogenēs ("alien-born," from allos — "other" + genos — "race/family") carries ethnic connotations that the Hebrew zar does not necessarily have: zar means "outsider" in terms of priestly status, not necessarily racial origin. A native Israelite non-priest is a zar; the LXX term implies foreign birth. This is a significant semantic narrowing that could mislead readers into thinking only ethnic foreigners are excluded.

**v.12 — bi-trumat ha-qodashim:** LXX *en tois aparchais ton hagiōn* — "in the firstfruits of the holy things." The LXX renders terumah as aparchai ("firstfruits"), the same term used for the first-fruits offering (bikkurim). This conflation of terumah (the heave-contribution lifted off from a sacrifice or produce) with aparchai (the firstfruits offering) blurs two distinct categories of holy contribution that the MT carefully distinguishes.

**v.14 — bi-shgagah:** LXX *akon* — "unwillingly/involuntarily." The Greek akon (from hekōn — "willing," with the privative a-) captures the inadvertent character of bi-shgagah. However, shgagah in Hebrew implies error or wandering from the known path, not merely lack of will; akon emphasizes the volitional dimension to the exclusion of the error dimension. Both concepts are present in the Hebrew; the Greek foregrounds one.

**v.18 — ish ish / min-ha-ger:** LXX *anthrōpos anthrōpos* — "a man, a man" — accurately renders the doubled ish ish. *ē apo tōn proselytōn tōn proskeimenoōn en Israēl* — "or from the proselytes who are attached in Israel." The LXX renders ger as prosēlytoi (proselytes), the term that later became technical for formal converts to Judaism. In the Levitical context, the ger is a resident alien who has not necessarily undergone formal conversion; the LXX's prosēlytoi reads a more developed religious-legal category back into the earlier text. This rendering is significant for NT studies as proselytes in Acts 2:10; 6:5; 13:43 likely reflects this LXX usage.

**v.19 — zachar tamim:** LXX *arsen amōmon* — "a male without blemish." The Greek amōmos (from a- + mōmos — "without blemish/fault") is the standard LXX rendering of tamim in sacrificial contexts. It becomes significant in NT Christology: 1 Pet 1:19 describes Christ as *amōmou kai aspilou* ("without blemish and without spot") against the background of this sacrificial requirement.

**v.21 — le-fallei neder:** LXX *euchen apodonai* — "to render/pay a vow." The LXX reads le-fallei neder as a payment of a vow (apodonai = "to give back/pay off"), which captures the obligatory character of the neder (once vowed, it must be paid) but loses the pala-root's connotation of something extraordinary or set-apart.

**v.23 — sarua ve-qalutt:** LXX *monokoton kai kolobon* — "one-eared and docked/shortened." The LXX appears to understand sarua as referring to one ear (monokoton — "having one ear/cut") and qalutt as a general shortening (kolobon — "docked/amputated"). This diverges significantly from the MT's limb-proportion interpretation. The Vaticanus reading is internally consistent but represents a genuine interpretive difference about the nature of these conditions.

**v.24 — machutz / kathit / nathiq / kharet:** LXX *molorchon ē tethlasmenon ē ektomian ē apokekopmena* — "castrated or crushed or excised or cut off." The LXX renders the four Hebrew terms with Greek equivalents that cover the same semantic range: molorchon (from morchōs — "withered/shrivelled"), tethlasmenon (from thlaō — "to crush"), ektomian (from ektome — "excision"), apokekopmena (from apokoptō — "to cut away"). The Greek sequence in Vaticanus is consistent with the progressive severity reading of the MT's four terms.

**v.27 — mi-yom ha-shemini va-hal'ah:** LXX *aph' hēmeras ogdoēs kai epekeina* — "from the eighth day and onwards." A precise rendering. The LXX accurately captures the temporal logic of the minimum age law. The eighth-day threshold is explicit in both MT and LXX.

**v.28 — oto ve-et-beno:** LXX *auto kai to paidion autou* — "it and its young." The Greek paidion (diminutive of pais — "child/young one") renders beno ("its son/offspring") with a term that emphasizes the youth and smallness of the offspring. The verse's compassion-logic is fully preserved in the LXX.

**v.32 — ve-niqdashhti be-tokh benei-Yisra'el:** LXX *kai hagiasthēsomai en mesō tōn hyiōn Israēl* — "and I shall be sanctified in the midst of the sons of Israel." The LXX renders the Nifal ve-niqdashhti as a future passive hagiasthēsomai ("I shall be sanctified/hallowed"), accurately capturing the Hebrew's passive-reflexive dimension. This verse becomes the basis for the Hebrew concept of kiddush ha-Shem (sanctification of the Name) as the counterpart to chillul ha-Shem.

**v.33 — ha-motsi etkhem me-erets Mitsrayim li-hyot lakhem l'Elohim:** LXX *ho exagagōn hymas ek gēs Aigyptou einai hymōn theos* — "who led you out of the land of Egypt to be your God." The LXX renders the closing formula with exact precision. The Exodus-grounding of the Holiness Code is fully preserved in the Greek, affirming that the LXX translators understood the theological weight of this closing verse as the foundation of the entire legislative complex.

---

*EMET translation completed. Leviticus 22:1–33. All 33 verses rendered. Source: Leningrad Codex folios 71r–71v. LXX: Vaticanus pp. 108–109.*
